biography:
midwest mom of 4 • author of a simpler motherhood 
simple pleasures • everyday magic • slow living • soaking in each season • making home a haven.

items.caption_text:
It’s been a while, hasn’t it?

I’ll be honest with you. I don’t really know how to show up in this space anymore. 

My love for writing, and sharing, and community run deep. And I adore a good scroll for inspiration, and an escape, and to ogle all the beautiful things. 

But since pursuing all things simple, I’ve found that time and time again, real life pulls me back, always providing a deeper motivation; a better escape; and infinitely more beautiful things than any social media feed could. 

And as I enter the next season of motherhood with all our kids in school (or preschool) and find pockets of time for myself, perhaps a post here and there will be enough. Or maybe it won’t. Who’s to say. But there is one thing I know holds true: this little corner of Instagram saved me as a struggling young mom in a way nothing else could. It provided people, and positivity, and a purpose when I felt overwhelmed with too much of everything else from this crazy, busy, more-is-always-better world. 

And for that, I’m eternally grateful.

I hope if you’re still here, you’re finding your own path to peace and contentment. I hope you find what brings you stillness, and slowness, and simplicity, and hold onto it for dear life.

I know the magnitude of joy it’s brought to my life. And I hope it’s doing the same thing for you. ❤️

items.caption_text:
Just popping on to say hello and remind you that slowing down can be a wonderful, glorious thing.

I know the rest of the world wants you to run the race, but in the stillness of this winter season, it’s more than okay to opt out and hunker down.

In fact, I highly recommend it.

Crank up that heat. Grab a cozy blanket. Make another cup of tea. Check out a good book at the library. Say no to any invite where sweatpants aren’t acceptable attire. And settle in for some rest and relaxation.

Seriously. I mean, more than any other month, I think January was made for it. ☕️

items.caption_text:
Hold on for dear life to the things that bring you joy, and let the rest go.

My short list of joys this Christmas season:

The Hallmark Channel Christmas lineup. 

Kenny G’s The Classic Christmas album on repeat. 

Pine scented candles, essential oils, soaps, lotions…pretty much anything. 

An eclectic, totally non-coordinating mix of sentimental, handmade, and Target purchased ornaments. 

Gingerbread creamer, cookies, coffee, you name it. Is there such a thing as too much gingerbread? If you ask me, the limit does not exist.

Any excuse to throw a string of twinkle lights. But the WARM white kind. Don’t get me started on the visceral reaction that sets in when I see a set of those cold, blue lights. 

Oh, and two real, messy, needle-dropping trees that may or may not make it to the finish line.

And…that’s about it, folks. You won’t find any “must-have” gift lists, handmade Christmas craft tutorials, elaborate, touch-it-and-you’ll-die decor, or “must-make” holiday recipes in this neck of the woods. 

Don’t get me wrong - I love all those things. But not enough to spend this wonderful, magical time producing content for the algorithm.

And I guess my hope is that maybe, just maybe, you’ll let go of all the expectations, lists, and “stuff” that this holiday season often ushers in on your feed.

Unless it brings you joy. In that case, add that to your plate and soak it up in all its glory.🎄

items.caption_text:
While it seems like every other human being on the planet is leaning HARD into the Christmas spirit, I hope I bring a breath of fresh, very-still-in-autumn air to your feed today.

Okay, I’m probably being a bit dramatic, but it seems to me that the frenetic pace at which we quite honestly plow through one season to get to the next one just so accurately depicts the way our world tends to live these days. And it makes me kind of - no, REALLY - sad.

Don’t take this as a personal knock if you’re already decking your halls and soaking in the twinkle lights. I get it - it’s utterly joy-inducing, and I don’t blame you for one second. Bring yourself that joy if that’s the road you want to travel.

But maybe, just maybe, there’s something to be said for the “waiting”. Maybe there’s a message this understated, slower autumn thing we’ve got going on right now is trying to share with all of us. And maybe, just maybe, if we slowed down for a second to sit in that quieter, calmer, not-so-excitingness that is most of this last autumn month, the beauty of the next season might just overwhelm us a bit more when it finally arrives.

Or maybe that’s just my $.02. Either way, I’ll be soaking in the crunch of the leaves, the porch stoop pumpkins, the crispy crunchy apples, and all things golden, red and orange for just a few more weeks. There’s always been a reason why autumn was my favorite season…and the more I slow down to soak it in, the more I find out why.

Here’s hoping maybe you are, too. 🍁

items.caption_text:
I stopped being a follower.

Maybe this post will be a little too intense for your early Sunday morning vibes. But I hope you read it anyway.

I’ve had this urge for years to unfollow everyone on social media. To remove the extra noise of passively consuming other people’s lives & to, instead, shift to actively pursing the content that fills my cup.

But I always had a reason not to. I might offend someone. I might lose my “following”. It would make it infinitely harder for me to find content I enjoy & worth consuming.

As a writer, it scared me a bit. As a human with a heart, it scared me more. And so I curated, muted, & limited my time on this little app. I worked hard to fit myself into the limits of what I “should” do for everyone else instead of having the courage to make this little virtual world one that made my happy; that served my needs.

This morning, I finally decided to take the plunge. It seems silly, talking about unfollowing people on social media like I deserve a badge of courage. But isn’t that the truth? And doesn’t that scare you? I mean, if the fear connected to an app on my phone has kept me from putting myself first, what does that say about the power this platform holds over me - and all of us?

But I’m also ashamed. I’ve written a book & preached the joys of simpler living to over 33,000 people for the last three years, & yet I’ve avoided doing the same kind of simplifying to the content I consume on here daily. That it’s taken this long for me to take that same leap of faith in the virtual world. That I have that much concern about what the world will think about what I choose to do or not do for my own happiness and peace.

Welp, not anymore. I hope if you’re new here, if you’ve been around since the beginning, or if you’re somewhere in-between, you understand. I hope you get it.

Actually, I hope you MORE than get it. I hope maybe this little square of my kitchen and these words strung somewhat haphazardly together stir something in your mind, your heart & your soul. I hope they open up the door for you, too, to actively SEEK the life you want instead of passively letting it come to you.

It may not be easy, but it’s always worth it.

items.caption_text:
Your time is THE most valuable asset you’ve been given. Spend it wisely.

 It’s been a while, I know. 

I’ve been noticing a shift in my priorities over the last months, and Instagram just isn’t it. Real life in this season is busy enough, and even if it wasn’t…spending my precious and hard-earned free time sharing my life with the world just isn’t my jam these days.

But nevertheless, I wanted to take a minute to hop on and share something that been in my mind. Unless you’ve even living under a rock, I bet you too have noticed this huge shift on social media from one of sharing and connecting to one of entertaining, and as a writer, it mildly terrifies me. 

I mean, when did we go from building communities to building brands?

When did things shift from sharing beautiful photos and ideas to sharing viral reels?

When did social media stop becoming a place of escape and start becoming a place of overwhelm?

I can’t be the only one feeling this way.

As autumn arrives at our doorsteps and we usher in this season of slowing down, I can’t help but wonder if the rest of the world will even try to follow in suit. More and more, the world (and social media) seems to operate at a constant, crazy, frenetic pace, and to be honest, I don’t want any part of it. It just doesn’t jive with my slower, more peaceful vibes anymore. 

I’ve always wanted less, but never more than as of late. Less stuff, less commitments, less pressure, less expectations. But it’s hard when you’re living in a world that constantly tells you that more is better. It’s hard to swim against the flow; to question the normal and buck the status quo. 

But the more I do, the better life gets. And I’m here to remind you that it’s okay to let go of it all and carve out your own simpler life, too - even when the rest of the world seems to be speeding along, accumulating more of everything these days. In fact, it’s more than just “okay”.

To me, it’s downright courageous. 

Also, on a totally different note: $20 worth of Trader Joe’s pumpkins, mums and pumpkin bread mix is all it takes to make your house feel like fall. 

You should try it.

items.caption_text:
You’ll never get this time back. 

You hear it a lot as a parent. “You only have 18 summers with your kids”. “Don’t blink”. “It goes so fast”. 

But guess what? 

They aren’t wrong. 

I’ve been doing a lot of reading - and a lot more thinking - lately. And it’s becoming frighteningly apparent to me that all this time we spend sharing on social media - it’s large chunks of our time I think we’re all going to really miss years from now. 

I don’t think a single one of us will ever look back and wish we would have shared more stories. Or scrolled our feed more. Or spent more time with our eyes glued to these addictive, glowing, dopamine-feeding screens.

But I’d be willing to bet that we absolutely will regret having a phone in our face, documenting our days or engrossed in the lives of someone else, while real life passes us by.

Do I think there’s a balance? Sure. Do I think most of us have found it? Hell no.

As I scroll my feed, I can’t help but wonder what’s being missed on the other side of those screens. I can’t unsee it or unthink it now. For every talking head documenting the latest cleaning hack, DIY or organized space, I can’t help but wonder who’s over there with them, behind the scenes, being ignored while strangers on the Internet get their precious time and attention.

And I can’t help but be motivated to opt out of all that whenever I can.

I’m not here trying to guilt you into change. Or judging you for your choices. But I AM here, pleading for you to take a nice long look in the mirror. I’m hoping that maybe these poorly organized thoughts of mine might open your eyes to the reality of your own world; that you, too, might no longer be able to unsee it. 

And I’m hoping, if nothing else, that you’re here, following along with me, for brief reminders to always choose your real life. To opt in to being present in the here-and-now. To prioritize your time and energy and spend it with the people who matter most. To constantly chase after that balance between your phone and your family - and to find the courage to change when things don’t feel right. 

Because I don’t think any of us will ever regret that.

items.caption_text:
I’m just over here, scrolling through set photos of Nancy Meyers movies, snacking on a still-warm-from-the-oven lemon bar and watching my two year old nap like an angel on the couch beside me while the Julie and Julia soundtrack plays in the background and the rest of my children are, by some miracle, playing quietly together. 

Life is pretty wonderful sometimes, isn’t it?

items.caption_text:
The prettiest little spice drawer you ever did see.

Okay, maybe not. I’m sure there’s a million more aesthetically pleasing labels, bottles and organizers out there. But I’m all about making things simple, so I just purchased a few sets of 4 oz mini mason jars and whipped out my old school label maker and called it a day.

Simple. Cheap. Pretty. That’s kinda my jam. 🫙 (Did you see what I did there?)

items.caption_text:
It’s a rainy Monday morning here. Which means watering all the new trees and shrubs we planted this year. And go figure - the minute I stick the hose on our new plants, the downpours come along. Isn’t that always how it goes?

I’m catching up on laundry, collecting our houseplants for a good soak, and praying that the upper 90’s heat wave that’s heading our way this week passes quickly. 🙏🏻

items.caption_text:
I LOVE a good, slow Sunday. Long, lazy chats over hot cups of coffee; cozy family breakfasts around our old, memory-laden dining table; a quiet hour at church (well, as quiet as it gets with four antsy kids who can’t ever seem to sit still). 

I’ve been spending this afternoon planning this week’s meals, getting the house back in order, lingering in bed as our youngest guy sweetly naps, and gearing up for another week of summer. It’s nothing grand; or even noteworthy to most. Sometimes I wonder why I even bother to share it. 

Yet it’s these slow, weekly rituals that feed my soul and make the ordinary feel extraordinary. And isn’t that what most of life is, really? A series of small, mundane, unimportant moments strung together that end up writing your story. 

I hope you’re finding the joy in your ordinary today, whatever it may be.

items.caption_text:
It’s one of those glorious sunny, post-swim naptime, freezer pizza, homemade popcorn and a movie, late bedtime kinda Fridays over here, and let me tell ya, I’m  reveling it all it’s gloriousness. TGI freaking F, amiright?

Anyone love a good unscheduled, unhurried summer as much as I do? It’s a whole vibe - and one that I hope to goodness that I never stop appreciating. ☀️

items.caption_text:
Really been loving this cute little curated corner of our kitchen this summer. Painting the cabinets a nice warm putty has given me all the cozy cottage vibes, and it’s made this kitchen such a beautiful, happy place to be. 

Also, I’ve embraced that change really is a good thing, and shifting the material that I choose I share on here is even better.

So going forward, plan on seeing shorter, sweeter and (hopefully) more regular posts from me, coupled with shots of my simpler spaces. Don’t be surprised if you notice an emphasis on the corners of our home that make me happiest.

Because doing what you love and sharing what brings you joy should always be priority. Facts. 

More to come soon. ❤️

More to come soon. ❤️

items.caption_text:
The moment still happens, whether you’re there for it or not.

I know motherhood can feel like one big never-ending to-do list. One endless season of waiting for the next, when things will be easier. One big continuum of people to care for, laundry to fold, and meals to make.

But it’s also one big season of life that you’ll never get back. Each day passes, and with it, so do those moments; preserved forever in the past.

Don’t let them happen without showing up for as many as you can. Because they’re going to happen, whether you’re “in” them or not.

Soak them up. The good; the bad; the ugly; the mundane. Because each one is writing your story for you…

whether you’re reading it or not.

Except those whole-family-stomach bug ones. I give you full permission to close your eyes and hold your breath for those bad boys.

Real life is pretty magical these days.

I don’t know about you, but until recently, I honestly couldn’t remember the last time I lived my life without documenting a minute of it - or wasting it watching someone else do the same. 

I couldn’t remember the last time I was able to put my phone down and not unconsciously pick it up without even knowing what I was looking for.

I couldn’t revel in the joy of a good moment or wallow in the disappointment of a tough day without wondering if it merited capturing its essence in a photo to share with the world.

I couldn’t avoid the constant discontent with my own home; my own accomplishments; my own parenting as I scrolled my feed hourly throughout the day, watching everyone else document their life in a constant, ever-growing highlight reel of DIY successes, clean-with-me reels, and beautifully decorated (and perfectly tidied) homes.

I couldn’t find the motivation to take the time to share my heart with the world, only to have the algorithms ruthlessly discard my carefully chosen words into the darkness.

And then I realized that none of that mattered. So I signed off. I checked out. And I stepped back into consuming and producing content for just me, in my real life - all day, every day. And it’s been glorious. Life-changing. Don’t know how (or why) I’d ever want to go back. 

So I thought I’d sign back on and take a few minutes to share what’s been on my heart with you in the hopes that, just maybe, you might have needed these words to gently urge you to do the same.

items.caption_text:
We lost our dog, Tyson this weekend, and there’s simply a gaping whole in my heart right now that just can’t be filled.

I’m not one to hop on here much lately, but I’d be remiss if I didn’t take the time to honor our guy with a decidedly important square on my feed, dedicated just to him.

Fifteen years of life with our furry guy was such a blessing, but also leaves such an aching gap in every room of this house; in every moment of my day. Pups just have a way of silently and steadily adding so much love and life into a home, and his absence certainly hangs heavily over me as I go about our day without him nestled into some cozy corner of the house.

Being a stay-at-home mom, he’s been my loyal shadow each day as I’ve gone about all the mundane tasks of motherhood and homekeeping. What a blessing to have him there, every day, to oversee our family as four new children have joined in over the years and added to the love and light among these walls. He’s been by my side through the best and worst of times, and words simply can’t express my love and gratitude for his steadfast presence through it all.

When he went, he took a piece of me with him for sure.

But also - he left so much behind, too. A lesson in loyalty, and unconditional love; a reminder to soak in the simple goodness that exists in every single beautiful day. 

And I’ll do my absolute best to honor him by living that way every chance I can get. ❤️

items.caption_text:
Less really always is more. At least in my book. 

It totally boggles my mind that after years of living simpler, I continue to find ways to remove the excess in my life.

In part, I wonder if that’s just a part of human nature. Things continually ebb and flow; space eternally fills and empties. Needs compound, then disappear. The seasons of motherhood - and of life - they continue to come and go, and with them, the needs do, too.

But one area of my life that’s taken an epically more minimal turn for the better in the last few months?

My time. 

Or rather, the demands on my time.

I’m wholeheartedly enjoying the freedom that comes with guarding my time with a more watchful eye. I’m feeling the peace that comes with slowing things down and single-tasking (is that even a real thing? And if not, can we please MAKE IT A THING!?) With adding space to think in, and around, and during the duties of my day. With welcoming silence, and thought, and reflection in, and shutting out the noise, and the the expectations, and the worry of the world whenever they come knocking (and man, are they persistent). 

And sometimes I wonder, with determined curiosity, if maybe that’s what we’re all really searching for in this minimalism movement after all. Not a less cluttered closet, or a perfectly organized pantry, or an easier-to-tidy home, but less of the clutter that fills the space around our stuff. Less of the running to-do lists and weighty expectations of the world. Less scrolling, and googling, and scheduling, and planning. Less watching, and waiting, and worrying. Just showing up in the moment, and taking each one as it comes, without pretense or expectation.

Maybe that’s what you’re after in this pursuit of simpler living, too. Because I sure know I am.

items.caption_text:
The kitchen cabinets are painted, and all of the sudden, this house feels a lot more like home. ❤️ That’s all the updates I’ve got for you guys for now - and somehow, that feels like just enough. 

Color: SW Sandbar

raw.caption:

items.caption_text:
Sometimes you just need to create space in your life. 

To paint your bathroom.
And your shutters.
And your kitchen cabinets.

To rearrange your laundry room.
And your linen closet. 
And your pantry.

To read.
And read some more.
And then read just a little bit more.

To re-examine habits.
And attitudes. 
And priorities.

To set aside, give up, and adjust my expectations on anything that’s just adding extra noise, clutter and stress to my days. 

To allocate my attention and time to the things; the stuff; the metrics in my life that REALLY, truly matter to me.

And to do it all without needing to share even a second of it with the world. 

And, when that’s all done, it’s even nicer to check back in with the assuredness that you can pick right back off where you left off, without skipping a beat.

It’s good to be back, guys. ❤️

items.caption_text:
It’s alright to savor the in-between.

I know your feed right now is slammed with all the decluttering, purging, and post-holiday motivation. Reel after post after story of clean, organized spaces, ready to start off the new year on a cleaner, tidier, foot. It’s inspiring, and encouraging - sure. But also, sometimes, it easily breeds discontent,  and feelings of inadequacy. Sometimes, it just makes you downright overwhelmed.

I’m here to remind you that you have a CHOICE. In the rush to start the new year off on just the right foot, you can hop on the bandwagon of onto-the-next…or you can step off the train and sit in all the peace and calm that comes with savoring the slowness of these quickly dwindling days of the post-holiday rush for just a little bit longer.

I bet you didn’t expect that from someone who’s written a book on simpler living, right? But see, that’s exactly WHY my life is simpler. I don’t feel the urgency to constantly chase after a more decluttered, more organized, more minimal home when there’s so much beautiful slowness and simplicity right around me to soak in.

The coming months will offer plenty of opportunity to clean out those closets; organize that pantry; declutter that storage room. But for now? You’ll find me with a mug of something hot, a good movie or a great book, curled up on the couch and soaking in the twinkling lights of our tree for just a few more days.

And if you’re feeling the urge to do the same, I hope you find the courage to set down your phone, put down the trash bag, and let the Christmas bins sit empty for just a few more days.

I have a feeling you won’t regret it.

items.caption_text:
In case you needed the reminder that most DIY projects don’t get done in a few days, that living in a work zone is completely normal, and that budgeting for the house of your dreams requires a lot of waiting, saving, and living through the imperfect…

Here’s our current in-progress kitchen. A lot of undone, messy and downright ugly. But I’ll take it if it means getting a more functional, more “me” kitchen that fits in our budget and works for our family. Because contrary to what’s out there, most real-life DIYs require a lot more time, planning, saving than what’s popping up on your daily feed. 

Double tap if you’re here for all the REAL real DIY projects in your feed (and maybe even your real life).

items.caption_text:
Crazy Christmas Mom isn’t a good look on anyone.

Trust me, I’ve been there. For years, I tried to make the holiday season fit into this perfect box I’d painted in my mind. Schedules full of Christmas activities, Martha Stewart approved holiday decor, and a whole lot of other crap that really didn’t make the holidays anything other than stressful, chaotic, expensive, and cluttered. 

So I tossed it all out. The guilt-induced activities; the bins of holiday decor; the self-imposed expectations I’d hoisted onto my already-heavy shoulders. I got rid of it all.

Well, most of it. 

And three years later, I don’t miss a thing.

I’m not here telling you to do the same thing - cause hey, we’re all living different lives. But in this season of extra, excess, and extravagance, maybe I’m just asking you to think twice before you add to your calendar, your head, your home, or your Amazon cart. 

And if you need me, I’ll be here, sniffing my tree often, keeping the lights on all day, and just taking in the Christmas season as a mom of young kids in all its wonderful, imperfect glory. And I’ll let the perfectionist in me chill out on making everything fit this pretty little picture I’ve got in my mind of how the holidays should be. 

Except for rearranging the ornaments. My OCD can’t handle when my kids “redecorate” the tree. 🥴 Anyone else with me on that one?

items.caption_text:
I don’t know about you, but fall cleaning is my JAM. I love a good deep clean of the house as things start to cool off, the holidays roll around, and I get ready to cozy up inside for the next few months.

Cause no one wants to spend their winter staring at dirty baseboards and windows, 
right?

Which is why I’ve been using The Simple Scrub to deep clean alllll the nooks and crannies of our new house. Things have never looked better - and I’m loving the pride that comes with loving on and maintaining all the character and charm of this old house, one little corner at a time. 

Wanna grab one for yourself? You can use promo code SIMPLIFIEDMOM15 for 15% off.

#thesimplescrub #simplescrub #cleanhome
#cleaningmotivation #cleaninghack #homekeeping #homekeeper #cleaningtips #housekeeping #homefortheholidays

items.caption_text:
You don’t have to do it all.

Let me repeat that: you don’t have to be everything, do everything or give everything. In fact, it’s perfectly fine to be, do, or give nothing more than YOU: your time, your presence, your sanity, your love, and your attention. Whatever you’re planning on doing, actually doing, or have already done for this season - and all the seasons to come - I promise, it’s enough.

With Halloween a week away, and the rest of the holidays just around the corner, this is your permission to let the to-do lists, expectations and should-dos go.

Do what you can. Do what matters to you and your people. Do what REALLY makes you happy. The rest?

Bye, Felicia. 

Just. Let. It. Go.

Because I can guarantee you won’t miss - or regret - a single one of those things that you wave goodbye. But do you know what you will regret?

Letting all those expectations and to-dos get in the way of actually enjoying your life; savoring the season; showing up for those beautiful moments with your people - for them, and for yourself.

So go tell your Pinterest boards, your Instagram feed, your over-eager Martha Stewart-esque neighbor, your overburdening aunt, and your own expectations to shove it. Shut it down - literally, and figuratively. Log off. Stop comparing. Just say no.

It’ll be the best new holiday tradition you ever adopt, I promise.

Double tap, comment or share if you’re with me- because I know I’m sure as heck not the only one ready to throw it all to the wind and actually enjoy my holidays this year.

items.caption_text:
I’ve been thinking a lot lately about what makes a house feel like “home”. 

And it’s made me realize how absolutely, incredibly essential the duties of making a home really are. 

Years ago, I used to be embarrassed that my entire job description began and ended with the duties of all things home and children. It didn’t feel like enough, although I’m not sure if that “enoughness” was my own insecurities, or simply what I imagined others thought.

But as we’ve painted walls, laid down rugs, deep cleaned floors, organized linen closets, arranged bedrooms, and unpacked boxes, I’ve realized how absolutely crucial my work is to the heart of this family.

Because making a home is absolutely one of the greatest labors of love a woman can bestow on her family.

Seeing my kids and my husband settle in as things become familiar and resume that safe and cozy vibe melts my heart in all the best ways. 

It’s not just having groceries in the fridge; clean clothes in drawers; freshly mopped floors to tread; or filling meals on the table.

It’s love, and security, and comfort in its most primitive, humble form. 

It’s far from the minutiae of a beautiful home and organized closets. It’s joy manifested through every minute of my day. Some sure are better than others, but it’s still there, just the same.

Homemaking - it’s an utterly beautiful thing. 

And whether it’s just a part of your job description, or it’s entirety- make sure you own all that beauty as often as you can.

items.caption_text:
Finally painted the living room walls.

After countless paint samples, stopping halfway through my first “sure” choice, and a whole week of keeping the littlest babe’s hands out of all the paint cans…she’s done.

And she’s perfect. Finally fell in love with Sherwin Williams’ Greek Villa, the perfect warm white for our old home, our golden oak floors, and my love of all things aged and vintage.

Now, to tackle the other 10 plus rooms in this house. Sigh. But really, it’s all about the journey, not the destination - isn’t it?

Isn’t everything, really?

items.caption_text:
Just dropping into your feed on this first day of fall to remind you that one simple pitcher of mums can be just as sweet as an entire house full of fall decor.

It doesn’t have to be all or nothing. Find you sweet spot when it comes to celebrating the seasons - and embrace it.

For me, that always means less. 

Less time. Less money. Less storage. Less hype. Less clutter. Less maintenance.

But also, more.

More time. More money. More space. More happiness. More peace. More freedom.

Because the best things about fall - or really, anything in life - are never the THINGS. 🍁

items.caption_text:
Let’s talk about contentment.

I have a confession. The perfectionist in me is really struggling with our new house right now. Like, really struggling.

Old homes are far from perfect. They come with a whole lot of charm…but often, a whole lot of problems, too. We’ve been running into what seems like one thing after another on a daily basis around here, and sometimes it feels like too much.

It’s hard to find contentment in outdated, old finishes that aren’t your style - sure. But it’s even harder to find appreciation for your home when you’re running into plumbing issues, and bigger problems - the not-so-fun things you know you’ll end up spending money on because they’re important, but that don’t provide a whole lot of visible return on your investment.

But the longer the to-do list becomes, the more I try to remind myself that it’s so worth it. Because taking care of the bones of a house - or a person, an animal, a relationship - matter far more than any cosmetic, pretty makeover we could provide.

And I’m hoping and praying this house’s old heart sure is grateful we decided to take a chance on her - and that she feels equally blessed to house a family who’s ready to nurture, care and restore her soul as her walls begin to do the same for us.

But it’s even harder to find appreciation for your home when you’re running into plumbing issues, and bigger problems - the not-so-fun things you know you’ll end up spending money on because they’re important, but that don’t provide a whole lot of visible return on your investment.

But the longer the to-do list becomes, the more I try to remind myself that it’s so worth it. Because taking care of the bones of a house - or a person, an animal, a relationship - matter far more than any cosmetic, pretty makeover we could provide.

And I’m hoping and praying this house’s old heart sure is grateful we decided to take a chance on her - and that she feels equally blessed to house a family who’s ready to nurture, care and restore her soul as her walls begin to do the same for us.

items.caption_text:
Good enough.

We moved into the new place three weeks ago now, and I severely underestimated the chaos of moving into a fixer upper with four little ones. Like, WAY underestimated it.

The rooms are smaller, and we chose to take the slightly tinier front bedroom on the main floor because it gets better light. (If you’ve been around here for a while, you know this is absolutely my priority 🤣). With that came the choice to get rid of our larger, taller bedframe. And since the whole first floor here is entirely comprised of these gorgeous golden hardwoods, we need a rug too. And a fresh coat of paint. And some patching on the walls. The list goes on and on.

I could hurriedly get all of that done for the sake of having a more “finished” space. But for now, I’ve chosen good enough. And let me tell you, it feels wonderful.

The bed is on the ground. The floors are a bit cool and squeaky. And the holes in the wall totally “make” the look. But I’m learning to slow down, and enjoy the process. To live in the spaces a bit, and learn what’s going to be just right for them. To sit with the undone, and channel my efforts into showing up for my kids, being present with my husband, and enjoying the beautiful wooded views out every window of this wonderful home.

Good enough. It really is a wonderful thing. Double tap, share or drop a comment if you’re in the same boat. ❤️
